en.st_wpn_ar15_freedom_descr:
A deeply modernized AR15 rifle, usually in service with the veterans of the Freedom. The body kit installed on the weapon allows you to feel confident at any firing range, but at the same time, the weapon is distinguished by a high price due to the use of very expensive components in the kit.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: All for picatinny/weaver (except of Red Dot Sights with low mount: Trijicon RMR, Leupold DeltaPoint PRO, Aimpoint T1 (low mount), Docter Sight and 1P76 Rakurs (picatinny).
  •  Installed Upgrades: B.E. MEYERS MAWL-C1 tactical green laser aim modul and Here Arms foregrip (helps in recoil control) and stock.
  •  Silencer: Surefire SOCOM556-MONSTER
en.st_wpn_ash12_descr:
The ASh-12.7 (АШ-12.7, which stands for "Автомат штурмовой 12.7мм" or "Automatic assault rifle, 12.7mm") battle rifle is a dedicated CQB/Urban Operations weapon, developed by TsKIB SOO ("Central Design and Research Bureau of Sporting and Hunting Arms"), a subsidiary of the KBP Instrument Design Bureau of Tula, Russia, by request from the Russian FSB (Federal Security Service). The weapon was designed with extreme short-range stopping power in mind because it is intended for special units of the FSB which operate in urban environments against heavily armed and organized gangsters and terrorists who need to be killed in one shot. According to the Russian press, the first batch of ASh-12.7 assault rifles was delivered to the FSB.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  ACOG, Barska ES AC10632, C-more Railway Tactical, EOTech scopes
en.st_wpn_aug_a1_bas_descr:
Steyr AUG A1 in standard rifle configuration (military green colour). The AUG is an Austrian bullpup 5.56mm assault rifle, designed in the 1960s by Steyr Mannlicher GmbH & Co KG (formerly Steyr-Daimler-Puch). The AUG (Armee-Universal-Gewehr "universal army rifle") was adopted by the Austrian Army as the StG 77 (Sturmgewehr 77) in 1978, where it replaced the 7.62mm StG 58 automatic rifle (a licence-built FN FAL). In production since 1978, it is the standard small arm of the Austrian Bundesheer and various national police units.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  1.5x scope (integrated).
  •  Suppressor: Steyr UMS
en.st_wpn_aug_a1_custom_bas_descr:
Steyr AUG A1 in standard rifle configuration (military green colour). The AUG is an Austrian bullpup 5.56mm assault rifle, designed in the 1960s by Steyr Mannlicher GmbH&Co KG (formerly Steyr-Daimler-Puch). The AUG (Armee-Universal-Gewehr "universal army rifle") was adopted by the Austrian Army as the StG 77 (Sturmgewehr 77) in 1978, where it replaced the 7.62mm StG 58 automatic rifle (a licence-built FN FAL). In production since 1978, it is the standard small arm of the Austrian Bundesheer and various national police units. CQB - has less weight and increased rate of fire compared to the original.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  1.5x scope (integrated).
  •  Suppressor: Steyr UMS
en.st_wpn_aug_a3_bas_descr:
Steyr AUG (Armee Universal Gewehr - Army Universal Rifle) is an Austrian 5.56x45mm NATO bullpup assault rifle, designed in the 1960s by Steyr-Daimler-Puch and now manufactured by Steyr Mannlicher GmbH & Co KG. The Steyr AUG A3 differs from the base model in the presence of four Picatinny slats: the top one is used for attaching sights, a grenade launcher or a vertical front handle can be attached to the bottom, and a laser pointer or other necessary additional equipment is attached to the side.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: All for picatinny/weaver except of scopes with very low mounting: Trijicon RMR, Leupold DeltaPoint PRO, Aimpoint T1 (low mount), Docter Sight.
  •  Suppressor: Steyr UMS
en.st_wpn_aug_a3_custom_bas_descr:
Steyr AUG (Armee Universal Gewehr - Army Universal Rifle) is an Austrian 5.56x45mm NATO bullpup assault rifle, designed in the 1960s by Steyr-Daimler-Puch and now manufactured by Steyr Mannlicher GmbH & Co KG. The Steyr AUG A3 differs from the base model in the presence of four Picatinny slats: the top one is used for attaching sights, a grenade launcher or a vertical front handle can be attached to the bottom, and a laser pointer or other necessary additional equipment is attached to the side.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Installed Trijicon Acog (x4) and PEQ LA5 LAM.
  •  Suppressor: Steyr UMS
en.st_wpn_aug_a3_descr:
Steyr AUG (Armee Universal Gewehr - Army Universal Rifle) is an Austrian 5.56x45mm NATO bullpup assault rifle, designed in the 1960s by Steyr-Daimler-Puch and now manufactured by Steyr Mannlicher GmbH & Co KG. The Steyr AUG A3 differs from the base model in the presence of four Picatinny slats: the top one is used for attaching sights, a grenade launcher or a vertical front handle can be attached to the bottom, and a laser pointer or other necessary additional equipment is attached to the side.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor
  •  ACOG, Barska ES AC10632, C-more Railway Tactical, EOTech scopes
en.st_wpn_aug_descr:
Steyr AUG A1 in standard rifle configuration (military green colour). The AUG is an Austrian bullpup 5.56mm assault rifle, designed in the 1960s by Steyr Mannlicher GmbH & Co KG (formerly Steyr-Daimler-Puch). The AUG (Armee-Universal-Gewehr "universal army rifle") was adopted by the Austrian Army as the StG 77 (Sturmgewehr 77) in 1978, where it replaced the 7.62mm StG 58 automatic rifle (a licence-built FN FAL). In production since 1978, it is the standard small arm of the Austrian Bundesheer and various national police units.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor
  •  1.5x scope (integrated).
en.st_wpn_aug_modern_descr:
Steyr AUG A1 in standard rifle configuration (military green colour). The AUG is an Austrian bullpup 5.56mm assault rifle, designed in the 1960s by Steyr Mannlicher GmbH & Co KG (formerly Steyr-Daimler-Puch). The AUG (Armee-Universal-Gewehr "universal army rifle") was adopted by the Austrian Army as the StG 77 (Sturmgewehr 77) in 1978, where it replaced the 7.62mm StG 58 automatic rifle (a licence-built FN FAL). In production since 1978, it is the standard small arm of the Austrian Bundesheer and various national police units. This model has been modified for usage of 7.62x51 NATO cartridge.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor
  •  1.5x scope (integrated).
en.st_wpn_beretta_descr:
The 92F is perhaps best known as the standard pistol of the US Military, replacing the legendary M1911. It is a short-recoil operated, locked-breech weapon with an aluminium frame and a hard chromed barrel bore. A popular sidearm adopted by several countries, it is especially popular among criminals for its high-powered rounds and high-capacity magazine.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  9x19 compatible suppressor (after modification)
en.st_wpn_bizon_descr:
This submachine gun has been developed in Izhevsk at the famous IZHMASH state arms factory during the early 1990s, first appearing to the public in 1993. Influenced by the Calico submachine guns, Bizon (Bison) SMG combined high capacity helical magazine with more conventional appearance and layout. The Bizon is built on shortened AKS-74 receivers, made from stamped steel. It featured a simple blowback action, hammer fired from closed bolt. The Bizon is capable of semi-automatic and automatic fire, with typical AK-type safety - selector lever on the right side of the receiver. Sights are similar to ones found on the AKS-74U, with hooded front post and flip up rear.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  EKP-8-02 Kobra, 1P29, PSO-1 scopes
  •  PBS-1 suppressor
en.st_wpn_bm16_descr: A double-barrelled shotgun with shortened barrels. Lighter and more compact, effective at close range. Needing a cheap, short range weapon that's effective and lethal, many rookie stalkers resort to sawing off the buttstock and barrel on the easily acquired TOZ 66. This makes the shotgun quick to wield, but at the cost of range and accuracy.\n \n
en.st_wpn_bm16_full_descr: A powerful weapon that every stalker can afford, TOZ 66 is a close range firearm possessing both reliability and effectiveness. However, being large and unwieldy, many stalkers prefer to spend their hard-earned rubles on a modern handgun instead.\n \n
en.st_wpn_colt1911_camo_descr:
The M1911 was the standard-issue side arm for the US military from 1911 to 1985. An extremely reliable and robust weapon, it's been adopted for military, police and civilian use around the world and remains a very popular firearm to this day. This is a unique model with red stains on dark-beige colour.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  .45 ACP suppressor (after modification)
en.st_wpn_colt1911_descr:
The M1911 was the standard-issue side arm for the US military from 1911 to 1985. An extremely reliable and robust weapon, it's been adopted for military, police and civilian use around the world and remains a very popular firearm to this day.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  .45 ACP suppressor (after modification)
en.st_wpn_colt1911_duty_descr:
The M1911 was the standard-issue side arm for the US military from 1911 to 1985. An extremely reliable and robust weapon, it's been adopted for military, police and civilian use around the world and remains a very popular firearm to this day. This variant has a distinctive feature - it's more powerful than the normal one.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  .45 ACP suppressor (after modification)
en.st_wpn_colt1911_merc_descr:
The M1911 was the standard-issue side arm for the US military from 1911 to 1985. An extremely reliable and robust weapon, it's been adopted for military, police and civilian use around the world and remains a very popular firearm to this day. This model is considerably more reliable and accurate.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  .45 ACP suppressor (after modification)
en.st_wpn_colt1911_n_descr: An improved version of a classic automatic pistol, which passed all the major armed conflicts of the 20th century with flying colours and entered the new millennium with confidence. Produced by dozens if not hundreds of arms manufacturers around the world in both standard and modified configurations. The weapon's only drawback, its mediocre magazine capacity, is largely compensated by the hard-hitting ammo it uses.\n
en.st_wpn_colt_kimber_descr: An advanced variant of the Colt M1911A1 developed by Kimber Manufacturing. The Desert Warrior was made for conducting special operations by the USA.
en.st_wpn_cz52_descr: The CZ 52 (also known by the Czechoslovak military designations vz. 52, for (vz. - vzor = model) "model of 1952", and CZ 482) is a semi-automatic pistol designed by two brothers, Jan and Jaroslav Kratochvil, in the early 1950s for the Czechoslovak military. Around 200,000 vz. 52s were made by Ceska Zbrojovka in Strakonice from 1952 to 1954. Before standardizing on the 7.62x25mm vz. 52, the Czechoslovak military used several domestic and foreign pistol models in three different calibers.\n \n
en.st_wpn_cz75_auto_descr: The CZ-75 was developed in the Czech Republic by the Koucky brothers. This semi-automatic pistol first appeared in 1975, entered production in 1976 and was intended for the export market. The CZ-75 accumulated the best features from many preceding designs and the resulting pistol was aesthetically attractive, comfortable to handle, shoot, accurate and reliable in operation. Made by CZ arms in various versions and modifications, the CZ-75 was also widely copied and cloned. The CZ-75 Auto is a 1995 model, boasting of a fully automatic fire mode and a longer barrel.
en.st_wpn_cz75_descr:
The CZ-75 was developed in the Czech Republic by the Koucky brothers. This semi-automatic pistol first appeared in 1975, entered production in 1976 and was intended for the export market. The CZ-75 accumulated the best features from many preceding designs and the resulting pistol was aesthetically attractive, comfortable to handle, shoot, accurate and reliable in operation. Made by CZ arms in various versions and modifications, the CZ-75 was also widely copied and cloned.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  9x19 compatible suppressor
en.st_wpn_desert_eagle_descr: The IMI Desert Eagle is a semi-automatic handgun notable for chambering the largest centrefire cartridge of any magazine fed, self-loading pistol and also being the heaviest and blockiest gun of its type. No one knows why IMI thought shooting a calibre of this size out of a handgun was a good idea, even though there are plenty of rounds out there that can do the job without breaking your wrist; this one assumes because of a pissing contest between the .44 and .50 AE. Not only is it heavy and harsh in recoil, you can also use it to bludgeon your enemies to death because of its blockiness and weight. Although militaries and law enforcement around the world, in their moment of sanity, never adopted this brick; Hollywood did, mostly because of its "cool factor" and now is the poster child for gangsters and pimps and a status symbol for those who reached a certain rank in a certain console shooter. Rumour is that there's a stalker with a crowbar wandering around the Zone with a rechambered version of this gun, but it's just a rumour.
en.st_wpn_desert_eagle_nimble_descr:
The IMI Desert Eagle is a semi-automatic handgun notable for chambering the largest centrefire cartridge of any magazine fed, self-loading pistol and also being the heaviest and blockiest gun of its type. No one knows why IMI thought shooting a calibre of this size out of a handgun was a good idea, even though there are plenty of rounds out there that can do the job without breaking your wrist; this one assumes because of a pissing contest between the .44 and .50 AE. This weapon was brought to the Zone by a stalker named Logan Williams. Disguised as a TIME photographer he managed to single-handedly enter the centre of Pripyat. His current whereabouts are unknown, but he is rumoured to work as an information broker specializing in all matters related to the Zone.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  DOCTER sight (integrated)
en.st_wpn_dvl10_descr:
DVL-10 is a lightweight and compact sniper rifle in proprietary and current military subsonic calibers has been developed for special service and combat missions where exceptional accuracy and range are needed.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: All scopes for picatinny/weaver except of red dot sights.
  •  Silencer: None
en.st_wpn_dvl10_m1_descr:
DVL-10 is a lightweight, compact, and silenced rifle in proprietary and current military subsonic calibers and has been developed for special service and combat missions where exceptional accuracy and range are needed.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: All scopes for picatinny/weaver except of red dot sights.
  •  Silencer: Integrated
en.st_wpn_eft_rsh12_descr: The powerful RSh-12 (Revolver Shturmovoy 12 - "Assault Revolver 12") revolver, manufactured by KBP Instrument Design Bureau, designed for use by special forces. Despite the powerful 12.7x55 mm caliber, RSh-12 has less recoil than many other large-caliber revolvers and pistols, since the shot is fired from the bottom chamber of the cylinder. \n \n
en.st_wpn_fal_aus_descr:
SA-58 manufactured by american company DSA (David Selveggio Arms) is a legal copy of the FAL, it's made with the same Steyr-Daimler-Puch production line equipment as the StG-58. This modelel comes with 21" barrel length, and other "Austrian Style" stuff like original Austrian foregrip for a FAL, aluminum-alloy lower receiver, and improved Glass-filled Nylon furniture. This model used by military and Law Enforcement and can run a fully automatic. The DSA-58 can use any metric-measurement FAL magazines.
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: None 
 
  •  Can be upgraded using Tactical Kits 
 
  •  Silencer: AAC 7.62 SDN-6
en.st_wpn_fal_aus_kit_aus_tri_descr:
FAL SA58 AUS modernised using DS Arms tactical kit. After the modification this weapon can be used very effectively at long and medium distances. SA-58 manufactured by american company DSA (David Selveggio Arms) is a legal copy of the FAL, it's made with the same Steyr-Daimler-Puch production line equipment as the StG-58. This modelel comes with 21" barrel length, and other "Austrian Style" stuff like original Austrian foregrip for a FAL, aluminum-alloy lower receiver, and improved Glass-filled Nylon furniture. This model used by military and Law Enforcement and can run a fully automatic. The DSA-58 can use any metric-measurement FAL magazines.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: Trijicon Acog and Aimpoint T1 (integrated)
 
  •  Silencer: AAC 7.62 SDN-6
en.st_wpn_fal_aus_kit_fal_leup_descr:
FAL SA58 AUS modernised using DS Arms tactical kit. After the modification this weapon can be used very effectively at long and medium distances. SA-58 manufactured by american company DSA (David Selveggio Arms) is a legal copy of the FAL, it's made with the same Steyr-Daimler-Puch production line equipment as the StG-58. This modelel comes with 21" barrel length, and other "Austrian Style" stuff like original Austrian foregrip for a FAL, aluminum-alloy lower receiver, and improved Glass-filled Nylon furniture. This model used by military and Law Enforcement and can run a fully automatic. The DSA-58 can use any metric-measurement FAL magazines.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: Leupold Mark 8 and Aimpoint T1 (integrated)
 
  •  Silencer: AAC 7.62 SDN-6
en.st_wpn_fal_aus_kit_sa5x_spec_descr:
FAL SA58 AUS modernised using DS Arms tactical kit. After the modification this weapon can be used very effectively at long and medium distances. SA-58 manufactured by american company DSA (David Selveggio Arms) is a legal copy of the FAL, it's made with the same Steyr-Daimler-Puch production line equipment as the StG-58. This modelel comes with 21" barrel length, and other "Austrian Style" stuff like original Austrian foregrip for a FAL, aluminum-alloy lower receiver, and improved Glass-filled Nylon furniture. This model used by military and Law Enforcement and can run a fully automatic. The DSA-58 can use any metric-measurement FAL magazines.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: ELCAN SpecterDr and Aimpoint T1 (integrated)
 
  •  Silencer: AAC 7.62 SDN-6
en.st_wpn_fal_descr:
FN FAL is a NATO firearm produced in Belgium by Fabrique Nationale de Herstal. One of the most recognized and popular automatic rifles. The FN FAL was originally developed for an intermediate 7.92x33 mm cartridge used by the Germans during World War II, then prototypes for the .280 British cartridge appeared. It was later remade chambered for 7.62x51 mm NATO, adopted as a single patron for NATO countries. During the Cold War, it received the nickname "the right hand of the free world."
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor
  •  ACOG, Barska ES AC10632, C-more Railway Tactical, EOTech scopes
en.st_wpn_fal_sa58_osw_descr:
SA-58 OSW (Operations Specialist Weapon) manufactured by American company DSA (David Selvaggio Arms) is a legal copy of the FAL, its made with the same Steyr-Daimler-Puch production line equipment as the StG-58. It may come with a different barrel length, an aluminum-alloy lower receiver, and improved Glass-filled Nylon furniture. Civilian models are semi-automatic, but military and Law Enforcement clients can procure select-fire models that have a fully automatic cyclic rate of 750 rounds/minute. The DSA-58 can use any metric-measurement FAL magazines.
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: All scopes for picatinny/weaver
  •  Silencer: AAC 7.62 SDN-6
en.st_wpn_famas3_descr:
FAMAS stands for Fusil d'Assaut de la Manufacture d'Armes de St-Etienne (Assault Rifle by St-Etienne Arms Factory). Development of this rifle began in 1967, by Paul Tellie, a French arms designer. This new rifle was intended to replace the ageing MAS Mle.49/56 semi-automatic rifles, MAT-49 submachine guns and MAC Mle.1929 light machine guns. The first FAMAS prototype had been built in 1971, and French military began to test this rifle in 1972. Meanwhile, France adopted a Swiss-designed SIG SG-540 5.56mm assault rifle as an intermediate solution. The FAMAS was adopted by the French in 1978 and since then became a standard French Army rifle, known among the French soldiers as "Le Clairon" (the bulge).
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor
  •  ACOG, Barska ES AC10632, EOTech scopes
en.st_wpn_fn2000_descr:
From the late 1980s to early 1990s, FN Herstal began to develop a new rifle. The aim was to produce a modern and modular weapon resulting in the FN F2000 rifle, first displayed to the public in 2001. The F2000 offers all of the popular features of the modern assault rifle such as a compact bullpup layout, ambidextrous handling and a modular design with plenty of options and addons available. So far, the FN F2000 rifle has found only a few buyers, including the Armed Forces of Slovenia and Belgian Special Operations Forces.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  FN2000 scope (integrated)
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor
en.st_wpn_fn2000_nimble_descr:
From the late 1980s to early 1990s, FN Herstal began to develop a new rifle. The aim was to produce a modern and modular weapon resulting in the FN F2000 rifle, first displayed to the public in 2001. The F2000 offers all of the popular features of the modern assault rifle such as a compact bullpup layout, ambidextrous handling and a modular design with plenty of options and addons available. So far, the FN F2000 rifle has found only a few buyers, including the Armed Forces of Slovenia and Belgian Special Operations Forces. This model has an integrated EOTech scope, which results in slightly lower accuracy, but since this model was aimed for CQC, rate of fire was increased accordingly.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  EOTech sight (integrated)
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor
en.st_wpn_fn57_bas_descr:
The Five-seveN is a delayed blowback operated, semi-automatic firearm. The Five-seveN pistol was designed by FN Herstal as a complimentary sidearm for the P90 submachine gun. The P90 and Five-seveN share the same ammunition, the 5.7x28mm SS190 cartridge. The idea behind the ammunition and weapon is to provide good penetration against targets protected by body armour, while keeping the weapon's weight, dimensions and recoil at reasonable levels.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  The kit includes Laser Aiming Module and Red Dot Sights mount
  •  Scopes: Docter Sight, Aimpoint T1 (low mount), Trijicon RMR, Leupold DeltaPoint PRO.
  •  Silencer: 5.7x28 suppressor
en.st_wpn_fn57_descr: The Five-seveN is a delayed blowback operated, semi-automatic firearm. The Five-seveN pistol was designed by FN Herstal as a complimentary sidearm for the P90 submachine gun. The P90 and Five-seveN share the same ammunition, the 5.7x28mm SS190 cartridge. The idea behind the ammunition and weapon is to provide good penetration against targets protected by body armour, while keeping the weapon's weight, dimensions and recoil at reasonable levels.
en.st_wpn_fnc_descr:
The FNC (Fabrique Nationale Carabine) is a 5.56x45mm NATO assault rifle developed by the Belgian arms manufacturer FN Herstal and introduced in the late 1970s.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor
en.st_wpn_fnp45_descr:
The FN FNP pistol is a series of semi-automatic, polymer framed pistols manufactured in Columbia, South Carolina by FNH USA, a division of Fabrique Nationale de Herstal. Debuted in early 2006, the FNP series weapons are hammer fired weapons utilizing a Browning cam system with an external extractor. The trigger module is housed inside the polymer frame as an individual unit connected to the hammer. The magazine release button is held in place by a retention pin which can be removed to allow the button to be reversed.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  GemTech suppressor
en.st_wpn_fnx45_descr:
The FNX line of pistols were introduced in 2009 by FNH USA, a US based subsidiary of the Belgian gun company FN Herstal. Originally developed to the requirements of the US Joint Combat Pistol Program, these pistols are manufactured in US and intended for military, law enforcement and civilian use. All FNX handguns are semi-automatic pistols with double/single action trigger and external hammer. Frame of the every FNX pistol is made from impact resistant polymer, with removable backstraps of different sizes. FNX-45 Tactical is a specialised variant of the basic FNX-45, that features extended threaded barrel and raised sights, to be used with detachable sound suppressor.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  FNX Tactical sight (integrated)
  •  GemTech suppressor (integrated)
en.st_wpn_fort500_descr: The Fort-500 smoothbore rifle was developed by the Fort NGO of the Ministry of Internal Affairs of Ukraine in the late 1990s, and was intended to arm the staff of the Ministry of Internal Affairs and security services of Ukraine. At present, the FORT-500 rifle in limited quantities is in service with the special units of the Ministry of Internal Affairs of Ukraine, and is also sold to citizens of this country in a hunting variant.\n \n
en.st_wpn_fort_descr:
The Fort-17 pistol is the latest development of the Ukrainian state owned FORT factory. It is a polymer framed modification of the earlier Fort-12 pistol, and all the mechanisms and properties are almost identical between the two pistols. However, the Fort-17 is lighter than its predecessor and features a removable grip backstrap, which can be had in various sizes, to adapt the pistol for a particular shooter.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  9x19 compatible suppressor
en.st_wpn_g28_descr:
The HK G28 rifle was developed by Heckler&Koch specifically for the Bundeswehr as a support weapon for small infantry units. The G28 is based on the HK MR308, which, in turn, is a civilian version of the HK 417 automatic rifle. Despite several differences, the HK G28 is 75% interchangeable with the HK 417. This rifle is designed to shoot at distances inaccessible to standard 5.56mm assault rifles.
 The Schmidt & Bender PM II 3-12x50 riflescope was originally created for elite military forces for high-quality target acquisition and pinpoint accuracy. The scope is outfitted with an illuminate Tremor2 reticle that offers a variety of markings to calculate precise shots while taking distance and windage into account.
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: Schmidt&Bender PM II 3-12x50 scope and Aimpoint T1 included.
  •  Suppressor: HK G28 B&T QD 7.62x51 sound suppressor.
en.st_wpn_g36_descr:
The H&K G-36 was known as the HK-50 project in early 1990s. After the cancellation of the G11 and G41 projects, the Bundeswehr was left with outdated G3 rifles; Heckler & Koch was set to develop a new assault rifle for both the German army and for export. The new 5.56x45mm assault rifle was adopted by the Bundeswehr in 1995 and also found its way into the hands of various law enforcement agencies worldwide, including British police and some US police departments. So far, there have been very few complaints about this rifle. The more common ones being the overheating of the handguards during sustained fire, and the loss of zero on the built in scope of G36KE rifles. Some German soldiers also complained about the position of the dual optical sights and it being easily fogged in bad weather. Otherwise, it is accurate, reliable and simple in maintenance.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor
en.st_wpn_g36k_descr:
A shortened variant of the G-36 rifle with a shorter barrel, fitted with an open type flash suppressor, a shorter forend and a bottom rail that can be used to attach tactical accessories, such as a UTL flashlight. The rifle's barrel lacks the ability to launch rifle grenades and will not support a bayonet. G36K in service with German special forces are issued with a 100 round C-Mag drum. There are two variants of the G-36K, first has 3x scope with carry handle attached to the top and the second is fitted with iron sights and rail.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  G36 scope (integrated)
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor
en.st_wpn_g3_descr:
The G3A3 is a battle rifle developed in the 1950s by the German arms manufacturer Heckler & Koch GmbH in collaboration with the Spanish state owned design and development agency CETME (Centro de Estudios Tecnicos de Materiales Especiales). It is designed to use the 7.62x51mm NATO round, and uses a Roller-delayed blowback action. The rifle owes its design to the CETME mod. A, which was designed by the german engineer Dr. Ludwig Vorgrimler. In 1957, Heckler & Koch bought the license to manufacture the rifle, where small modifications were made to the CETME design. Officially adopted into service in 1959, by the Bundeswehr as the G3, it remained in service until 1995 when it was replaced by the G36. Despite having been officially replaced by the German army, it is still in limited use.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor
  •  ACOG, Barska ES AC10632, EOTech scopes
en.st_wpn_g3sg1_descr:
A sniper variant of the G3. The "SG" stands for Scharfschutzengewehr, or "sharpshooting rifle". The rifles were individually selected from the production line for their accuracy and then modified. A Zeiss 1.5-6x variable power telescopic sight was added using a HK claw mount attached to the receiver. The stock was extended slightly and comes with a heavy, dual-stage buffer and adjustable cheek rest. A special set trigger group was added for a crisp trigger pull of 1 lb. Automatic fire was retained.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  G3 scope (integrated)
en.st_wpn_g43_descr:
Adopting a simple gas-operated mechanism from Soviet SVT rifles, Nazi Germany developed the Gewehr 43 semi-automatic rifle. It was renamed Karabiner 43 in 1944. Equipped with the ZF 4 telescopic sight, it is an effective sniper rifle.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  ZF-41 scope (integrated)
en.st_wpn_galil_descr:
Formally accepted into service with the IDF in 1972, the IMI Galil is designed for operation in arid conditions and is based on the Finnish RK 62, which itself was derived from the Soviet AK-47. The Galil is noted for its reliability in combat conditions, and ease of maintenance. It has a line chambered for 5.56x45mm and one for 7.62x51mm.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  PBS-1 suppressor
  •  Galil 4x scope (after modification)
en.st_wpn_gauss_bas_descr:
A highly experimental, artefact-powered, super-accurate weapon made by an unknown party in the Zone. Its gunshot is nearly inaudible, because instead of gunpowder, it uses magnets to accelerate its bullet to incredible speeds. Despite the gauss rifle's tremendous firepower, the recoil is insignificant. The accuracy and power of this gun exceeds those of all other small arms.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Enhanced computer EOTS-62 scope (integrated)
en.st_wpn_glock17_descr:
The Glock 17 is a polymer-framed, short recoil-operated, locked-breech semi-automatic pistol designed and produced by Austrian manufacturer Glock Ges.m.b.H.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: None
  •  Silencer: None
en.st_wpn_glock17_m1_descr:
The Glock 17 is a polymer-framed, short recoil-operated, locked-breech semi-automatic pistol designed and produced by Austrian manufacturer Glock Ges.m.b.H. The tactical variant of Glock 17 has some additional futures: integrated Aimtech glock base for Red Dot sights, installed Pachmayr tactical rubber grip and threaded barrel manufactured by Double Diamond.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: Docter Sight, Aimpoint T1 (low mount only), Trijicon RMR, Leupold Deltapoint PRO
  •  Silencer: Osprey 9mm
en.st_wpn_glock_descr:
The Glock 19 is a smaller version of the Glock 17. Its reduced size made the Glock 19 easier to conceal and carry by military and police forces. The pistol was debuted by Glock GbmH of Austria in 1988. When compared to the Glock 17, the Glock 19 retains the weight of its slide to keep the short recoil operation in check while at the same time shortening the grip and barrel. Many components of the Glock 19 were made interchangeable with the Glock 17, including the magazine. Simply put, the Glock 19 is a compact version of the 17.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  GemTech suppressor (after modification)
en.st_wpn_glock_modern_descr:
The Glock 19 is a smaller version of the Glock 17. Its reduced size made the Glock 19 easier to conceal and carry by military and police forces. The pistol was debuted by Glock GbmH of Austria in 1988. When compared to the Glock 17, the Glock 19 retains the weight of its slide to keep the short recoil operation in check while at the same time shortening the grip and barrel. Many components of the Glock 19 were made interchangeable with the Glock 17, including the magazine. Simply put, the Glock 19 is a compact version of the 17. This model has an optional automatic fire mode.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  GemTech suppressor (after modification)
en.st_wpn_grach_descr: The MP-443 is a short recoil operated, locked breech pistol that was developed for the Russian Army trials, codenamed "Grach" (rook). These trials were started by issuing initial requirements for a new military pistol in 1993. Several designs were submitted to the trials and the winner was the pistol designed at the state Izhevsk Mechanical Plant by Vladimir Yarygin. This pistol, initially known as the 6P35 military index during trials or the MP-443 "Grach" manufacturer's designation, is adopted by the Russian Government for military and law enforcement use under the PYa designation, and already issued to some Russian Army units.
en.st_wpn_groza_descr:
The OTs-14 "Groza" ("Thunder") modular assault rifle was developed during the early 1990s at the TSKIB SOO. It was intended for various special forces in the Russian Army and Internal Affairs Ministry as an dedicated urban warfare weapon. Briefly manufactured in small numbers at the Tula Arms factory during the mid-1990s, the OTs-14 saw action in Chechnya in 1999, but fell out of favour and are no longer being made. It is a bullpup design, with removable foregrip which can be replaced with a 40mm grenade launcher. Adopted for Spetsnaz forces and specialist front-line combat units, the OTs-14-4A allows for assembly of one of four weapon versions, depending on the mission. This Zone version is equipped with the GP-25 grenade launcher. 
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  PO 4x34 scope
  •  GP-25 grenade launcher (integrated)
en.st_wpn_groza_nimble_descr:
Being the only variant of the OTs-14-4A, the OTs-14-1A "Groza-1" is chambered for 7.62x39mm. Fire rate, flatness and magazine capacity are improved thanks to the use of this lighter, faster round. The Groza-1 was only produced in small batches.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  PO4x34 scope
  •  GP-25 grenade launcher (integrated)
en.st_wpn_gsh18_descr: The GSh-18 is a 9x19mm semi-automatic pistol developed in the early 1990s at the KBP Instrument Design Bureau in Tula. The pistol's name is derived from its designers - Gryazev and Shipunow, and the number 18 denotes the magazine capacity. The GSh-18 is a recoil-operated, locked breech pistol. The weapon has an unusual cam-rotated barrel with 10 locking lugs. The GSh-18 is striker fired and features a pre-set trigger that pre-cocks the pistol when the slide recoils after firing a shot, the striker firing pin is then fully cocked by pulling the trigger back to the rear. With the 7N21 and 7N31 armour-piercing bullets, it can pierce 8mm of steel at 20 metres.
en.st_wpn_hk416_descr:
The HK416 is a rifle designed and manufactured by Heckler & Koch to improve the M4. It is available as a complete firearm, or as an upper receiver that fits on any AR-15 type lower receiver. The US Army's Delta Force collaborated with the German arms makers to develop the new rifle. Testing the H&K 416 in 2004 revealed that the piston operating system significantly reduces malfunctions while increasing the life of parts. The project was originally called the H&K M4, but was changed in response to a trademark infringement suit filed by Colt Defence. Currently, it is only available to government and military organisations, hence why this assault rifle is usually carried by Mercenaries.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor
  •  ACOG, Barska ES AC10632, EOTech scopes
  •  M203 grenade launcher
en.st_wpn_hk417_descr:
The Heckler & Koch HK417 is a battle rifle designed and manufactured by Heckler & Koch in Germany. It is the larger caliber version of the HK416, and chambered for the full-power 7.62x51mm NATO rifle cartridge. It is a gas-operated, selective fire rifle with a rotating bolt. The HK417 is intended for use in roles where the greater penetrative power, stopping power, and range of the 7.62x51mm cartridge are required. It has been adopted for service by a number of armed forces, special forces, and police organizations.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor
  •  ACOG, EOTech scopes
en.st_wpn_howa20_descr:
The assault rifle was officially unveiled to the public in May 2020. This weapon is intended to replace the Type 89 assault rifle, which has been in service for about 30 years. The new rifle of the Japanese Self-Defense Forces, like the previous one, was developed by Howa Machinery Co, and uses 5.56 mm ammunition. The development of a new rifle to replace the Type 89 began at the request of the Japanese Armed Forces in 2014. Externally and structurally, the new Type 20 rifle is a kind of arithmetic mean of many modern rifles, such as the HK433 or CZ BREN II, and, apparently, does not have any original features. However, there is nothing wrong with this, since it is not the originality of the design that is required from the army weapon, but reliability, low price and compliance with the customer's requirements. It remains a mystery how and in what quantity this rifle came to the participants of a scientific expedition to the Zone.
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Includes: MARCH Shorty scope and AN/PEQ-15A DBAL-A2 Laser Device
  •  Silencer: KAC QDSS NT-4 5.56х45
en.st_wpn_hpsa_descr:
The Browning Hi-Power is a single-action, 9mm semi-automatic pistol. It is based on the ideas of American firearms inventor John Browning, created and patented in 1922, later patented by FN Herstal. Browning died in 1926, before he had finished developing a production version, but FN arms designer Dieudonne Saive fully developed it. The Hi-Power pistol was noted for its 13 round magazine capacity, twice that of contemporary designs such as the Luger or Mauser 1910. This modified variant of the Browning HP pistol, despite its venerable age, enjoys popularity around the world.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  9x19 compatible suppressor (after modification)
en.st_wpn_k98_descr:
During World War II, certain Karabiner 98k rifles were selected for their exceptional accuracy and fitted with the ZF39 telescopic sight. These rifles were used by Nazi Germany as sniper rifles. In skilled hands, they had an effective range of up to 1000 metres.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  ZF scope series
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor (alternate)
en.st_wpn_k98_mod_descr:
A modified version of the WWII German sniper rifle Mauser Karabiner 98k. The wooden stock was replaced with a more organomic polymer one. The rifle is equipped with an excellent sniper scope manufactured by March, Japan. In the Zone, a rifle is used mainly for hunting mutants at long and over-long distances.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: March (integrated)
  •  Silencer: K98 Forester. (Barrel adapter needed to mount a silencer)
en.st_wpn_k98_mod_silen98_descr:
A modified version of the WWII German sniper rifle Mauser Karabiner 98k. The wooden stock was replaced with a more organomic polymer one. The rifle is equipped with an excellent sniper scope manufactured by March, Japan. In the Zone, a rifle is used mainly for hunting mutants at long and over-long distances.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: March (integrated)
  •  Silencer: K98 Forester.
en.st_wpn_kar98_retrotac_descr:
Modified version of the German WWII sniper rifle Mauser Karabiner 98k - another work of the Forester, known for his love for the restoration and modernization of old captured weapons of the WWII era. The original wooden stock has been slightly lightened; a handguard is installed on its front part, allowing the installation of an additional night sight. The rifle is equipped with an excellent sniper scope manufactured by March, Japan. According to the Forester, this rifle is more suitable for hunting intruders in the Red Forest than mutants.
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: March (included), PN-23 "Nozzle" 
  •  Suppressor: K98 Forester.
en.st_wpn_karabiner98k_descr:
During World War II, certain Karabiner 98k rifles were selected for their exceptional accuracy and fitted with the ZF39 telescopic sight. These rifles were used by Nazi Germany as sniper rifles. In skilled hands, they had an effective range of up to 1000 metres. 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Can be upgraded using Tactical Kit (silencer adapter).
  •  Scopes: ZF-4 (included).
  •  Suppressor: None (Barrel adapter kit needed to mount a silencer)
en.st_wpn_karabiner98k_silen98_descr:
During World War II, certain Karabiner 98k rifles were selected for their exceptional accuracy and fitted with the ZF39 telescopic sight. These rifles were used by Nazi Germany as sniper rifles. In skilled hands, they had an effective range of up to 1000 metres. 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: ZF-4 (included).
  •  Suppressor: K98 Forester.
en.st_wpn_kiparis_descr:
OTs-02 "Kiparis" (Cypress) is a sub-machine gun, developed in 1972 by order of the Ministry of Defence of the USSR by weapon designers from TsKIB COO of Tula. The layout is based on the Czechoslovak sub-machine gun Scorpion Vz.61 (1961 variant). The OTs-02 "Kiparis" automatic mechanism is powered by a free bolt recoil. Shots are fired from closed bolt, which increases the accuracy of fire. To increase stability of the weapon, a folding stock is used, which in the compact position is folded on top of the receiver. In the Zone, this weapon is not uncommon and usually used by bandits and loners who can't afford a better weapon.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  9x19 compatible suppressor
en.st_wpn_kiparis_m1_descr:
OTs-02 "Kiparis" (Cypress) is a sub-machine gun, developed in 1972 by order of the Ministry of Defence of the USSR by weapon designers from TsKIB COO of Tula. The layout is based on the Czechoslovak sub-machine gun Scorpion Vz.61 (1961 variant). The OTs-02 "Kiparis" automatic mechanism is powered by a free bolt recoil. Shots are fired from closed bolt, which increases the accuracy of fire. To increase stability of the weapon, a folding stock is used, which in the compact position is folded on top of the receiver. In the Zone, this weapon is not uncommon and usually used by bandits and loners who can't afford a better weapon.
 Mechanic Polimer, as it turned out, considers himself a true fun of the old, but very affordable cartridge 9x18 in the southern regions of the Zone. The line of inexpensive submachine guns he developed is very popular among stalkers in all fuctions in Zone. This model differs from the standard mainly in some simple attachments and slightly increased rate of fire.
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  The weapons are equipped with: the cheapest of all possible laser sight and a picatinny rail for attaching some small sights.(Also, as a bonus, Polymer gives you a set of 40 round mags when you buying it).
  •  Scopes: All Red Dot Sights for picatinny/weaver except of scopes with long mount such as: Eotech 553/XPS, C-More, OKP-7, Meprolight M21, EKP-8-18 and Kemper.
  •  Suppressor: 0217 Sound Suppressor.
en.st_wpn_kiparis_m2_descr:
OTs-02 "Kiparis" (Cypress) is a sub-machine gun, developed in 1972 by order of the Ministry of Defence of the USSR by weapon designers from TsKIB COO of Tula. The layout is based on the Czechoslovak sub-machine gun Scorpion Vz.61 (1961 variant). The OTs-02 "Kiparis" automatic mechanism is powered by a free bolt recoil. Shots are fired from closed bolt, which increases the accuracy of fire. To increase stability of the weapon, a folding stock is used, which in the compact position is folded on top of the receiver. In the Zone, this weapon is not uncommon and usually used by bandits and loners who can't afford a better weapon.
 Mechanic Polimer, as it turned out, considers himself a true fun of the old, but very affordable cartridge 9x18 in the southern regions of the Zone. The line of inexpensive submachine guns he developed is very popular among stalkers in all fuctions in Zone. This model was made by Polymer for Duty faction.
 This model differs from the standard mainly in some simple attachments and slightly increased rate of fire. Also this model comes with handcrafted Bakelite foregrip which increases the recoil control. The Bakelite grip is attached to steel brace connected to the buttom of the Kiparis's fore-end and reinforced with three titanium screws.
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  The weapons are equipped with: laser sight and, picatinny rail for attaching some small sights and handcrafted Bakelite foregrip. (Also, as a bonus, Polymer gives you a set of 40 round mags when you buying it).
  •  Scopes: Trijicon Acog and Red Dot Sights for picatinny/weaver except of scopes with long mount such as: Eotech 553, C-More, Meprolight M21, EKP-8-18.
  •  Suppressor: 0217 Sound Suppressor.

 This model differs from the standard mainly in some simple attachments and slightly increased rate of fire. The Bakelite grip is attached to steel brace connected to the buttom of the Kiparis's fore-end and reinforced with three titanium screws.

In the Zone, this weapon is not uncommon and usually used by bandits and loners who can't afford a better weapon.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Suppressor: 0217 Sound Suppressor.
en.st_wpn_korth_custom_descr:
Korth is a high-end firearms manufacturer based in Lollar, Hesse, Germany. Korth is a high-end firearms manufacturer based in Lollar, Hesse, Germany. Korth. For some gun aficionados, nothing more needs to be said. Builders in the tradition of ultimate European luxury brands, Korth are perhaps best known for revolvers with price tags that most people would find easier to associate with German cars than German guns. But that out-of-this-world pricing reputation which is the stuff of gun counter lore world wide is derived from their full custom stuff; the entry point is far more accessible. The starting point for a Korth is around the starting point for a Rolex: five thousand dollars will put some of that European goodness in your hands, and it will sure be memorable. Korth guns are legendary.
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Installed long 6 inch threaded barrel.
  •  Scopes: Docter Sight
  •  Silencer: BLACK BOX .45

en.st_wpn_kriss_cust_descr:
KRISS Vector is a family of modern submachine guns developed by the American company KRISS USA. They use an unconventional delayed blowback system combined with in-line design to reduce felt recoil and muzzle climb of the weapon. The Vector shoots with incredibly high cyclic rate of 1200 rounds per minute, but the recoil reducing mechanism makes it easy to control. In The Zone it is used by mercenary groups and loners who need a compact and light weapon for use as PDW, but due to it's extremely high rate of fire it is also used as weapon for mutant hunting - even short burst can send lots of bullets down range, increasing the chance of critical hits. This model includes a vertical grip and LAM Module, making it better in CQB.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  RVG Vertical Grip 
  •  L3Harris LA-5B/PEQ tactical device 
  •  SilencerCo Omega .45 caliber suppressor
en.st_wpn_ks23_23_up_descr: Special carbine, 23 mm - Soviet multifunctional police pump-action shotgun, designed to suppress riots, selective force, mental and chemical effects on offenders. Interestingly, having a caliber of more than 20 mm and a rifled barrel, the KS-23 should not be classified as small arms, but as small-caliber artillery. This modification of KS-23 has short 510mm barrel installed on it instead of standard long 700mm one.\n
en.st_wpn_ks23_descr:
Special carbine, 23 mm - Soviet multifunctional police pump-action shotgun, designed to suppress riots, selective force, mental and chemical effects on offenders. Interestingly, having a caliber of more than 20 mm and a rifled barrel, the KS-23 should not be classified as small arms, but as small-caliber artillery.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: The long 700mm barrel can be replaced by short 510mm one to decrease the wheight of the weapon.
en.st_wpn_ks23_kaban_descr:
Shortened version of KS-23 with removable metal stock. 23 mm - Soviet multifunctional police pump-action shotgun, designed to suppress riots, selective force, mental and chemical effects on offenders. Interestingly, having a caliber of more than 20 mm and a rifled barrel, the KS-23 should not be classified as small arms, but as small-caliber artillery.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: Boar Tactical Kit for KS-23M Drozd.
en.st_wpn_ks23_kaban_kab_up_descr: Shortened version with mounted Trijicon RMR reflex sight. 23 mm - Soviet multifunctional police pump-action shotgun, designed to suppress riots, selective force, mental and chemical effects on offenders. Interestingly, having a caliber of more than 20 mm and a rifled barrel, the KS-23 should not be classified as small arms, but as small-caliber artillery. Also this version has thread for sound supressor that can be found somewere at south locations of Zone.\n
en.st_wpn_l85_descr:
The development of the SA80 system, which included two weapons - the SA80 IW assault rifle and SA80 LSW light machine gun, began in the late 1960s when the British Army decided to develop a new rifle, to replace the ageing 7.62x51mm L1 SLR British made FN FAL. The standard individual weapon for the British armed forces, the L85 (in its original A1 version) had numerous performance issues. The improved L85A1 is extremely reliable over a range of climates for various operational scenarios. The current L85A1 rifles are recognised as reliable and accurate, especially when using standard issue SUSAT telescope sights.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  SUSAT scope (integrated)
  •  5.56x45 compatible suppressor (after modification)
en.st_wpn_l85_m1_descr:
The development of the SA80 system, which included two weapons - the SA80 IW assault rifle and SA80 LSW light machine gun, began in the late 1960s when the British Army decided to develop a new rifle, to replace the ageing 7.62x51mm L1 SLR British made FN FAL. The standard individual weapon for the British armed forces, the L85 (in its original A1 version) had numerous performance issues. The A2 variant came to be as the result of a significant upgrade in the early 2000s by Heckler & Koch and remains in service as of 2020. L85A2 is extremely reliable over a range of climates for various operational scenarios. The current L85A2 rifles are recognised as reliable and accurate, especially when using standard issue SUSAT telescope sights.
 
 ATTACHMENTS: 
  •  Scopes: Susat with L85 native mount
  •  Suppressor: ADVANCED ARMAMENT (AAC) M4-2000
  •  Grenade Launcher: none

  •  Suppressor: ADVANCED ARMAMENT (AAC) M4-2000
  •  Grenade Launcher: none
